elements.sent-2411-001: This paper explores the application and effectiveness of Test-Time Training (TTT) layers in improving the performance of recommendation systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: We developed a model, TTT4Rec, utilizing TTT-Linear as the feature extraction layer.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our tests across multiple datasets indicate that TTT4Rec, as a base model, performs comparably or even surpasses other baseline models in similar environments.
elements.sent-2411-004: The rapid advancement of deep learning has significantly influenced the development of recommendation systems, leading to the emergence of several prominent sequential recommendation models, including DIN\cite{ref:din , GRU4Rec\cite{ref:gru4rec , SASRec\cite{ref:sasrec , Bert4Rec\cite{sun2019bert4rec , ComiRec\cite{ref:comirec , ICLRec\cite{chen2022intent , and so on.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: With the advancement of technology and actual demand, recommendation technology has begun to develop toward personalization\cite{lv2023duet,lv2024semantic,lv2024intelligent,sun2022response , multimodality\cite{zhang2021mining,zhang2022latent,zhang2023mining,ji2023online , privacy protection\cite{liao2023ppgencdr , cold-start\cite{liu2023joint,chen2021improving , session-based\cite{su2023enhancing , disentangle\cite{chen2021deep , LLM-based\cite{zhang2023collm,bao2023tallrec , generative-based\cite{zhao2024denoising,lin2024efficient , and so on.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Recently, a novel architecture known as Test-Time-Training (TTT) layers\cite{ref:ttt has been proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-007: This architecture employs self-supervised learning to update the weights of hidden states by performing gradient descent for each token.
elements.sent-2411-008: Two variants of TTT have been introduced: TTT-Linear and TTT-MLP, with TTT-Linear demonstrating superior performance and efficiency in short-context scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-009: Given the short-context nature and high-efficiency demands of recommendation systems, we conducted a preliminary exploration of TTT-Linear's performance within this context.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this preliminary exploration, we developed a simple but effective sequential recommendation model, TTT4Rec, utilizing TTT-Linear as the feature extraction layer.
elements.sent-2411-011: TTT's central idea leverages self-supervised learning to compress historical context \(x_1, \ldots, x_t\) into a hidden state \(s_t\).
elements.sent-2411-012: Here, the context is treated as an unlabeled dataset and the state as a model.
elements.sent-2411-013: The hidden state \(s_t\) corresponds to \(W_t\), the weights of a model \(f\), which can be a linear model, a small neural network, or another form.
elements.sent-2411-014: The output is defined as: \begin{equation}z_t = f(x_t; W_t)\end{equation} The output token represents the prediction on \(x_t\) made by \(f\) with the updated weights \(W_t\).
elements.sent-2411-015: The update rule is a step of gradient descent on a self-supervised loss \(\ell\): \begin{equation}W_t = W_{t-1} - \eta \nabla \ell(W_{t-1}; x_t)\end{equation} where \(\eta\) is the learning rate.
elements.sent-2411-016: The algorithm maps an input sequence \(x_1, \ldots, x_T\) to an output sequence \(z_1, \ldots, z_T\) using the hidden state, update rule, and output rule described above.
elements.sent-2411-017: Even during testing, the new layer trains a unique sequence of weights \(W_1, \ldots, W_T\) for each input sequence.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.86\linewidth]{images/model.jpg} \caption{Model Architecture of TTT-Linear in Recommendation System} \label{fig:TTT4Rec}
elements.sent-2411-018: We define a data instance as \(\mathcal{X = \{u, v, s\}\) and the corresponding label as \(\mathcal{Y = \{y\}\), where \(u\) represents the user ID, \(v\) denotes the item ID, and \(s\) indicates the user's click sequence.Figure\ref{fig:TTT4Rec illustrates the architecture of TTT4Rec.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: For each item ID \(v_i\), latent representations are obtained through an embedding layer.
elements.sent-2411-020: This layer processes \(v_i\) to produce its ID embedding \(e_v \in \mathcal{R ^K\).}
elements.sent-2411-021: The extracted features are subsequently normalized using RMSNorm, and the hidden state \( F_s \) corresponding to the last valid click is output.
elements.sent-2411-022: This embedding is then fed into a two-layer MLP to extract the target features \(F_t\).
elements.sent-2411-023: Finally, the dot product of \(F_t\) with the click sequence features \(F_s\) yields the predicted click probability for the target. \begin{equation}R = F_s \cdot F_t\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-024: Datasets: Our evaluation was carried out on three widely recognized datasets frequently used in recommendation research: Amazon Beauty, Amazon Electronics, and MovieLens-1M.
elements.sent-2411-025: For negative sampling, we adopted a 1:4 ratio during the training phase, where each positive instance is paired with four negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-026: In contrast, a 1:99 ratio was applied during the testing phase to closely approximate real-world recommendation scenarios, enhancing the robustness of our model's performance assessment.
elements.sent-2411-027: To fairly assess the performance, the experiments were conducted with a learning rate of 0.001 and 10 training epochs(50 epochs for ComiRec).
elements.figure-2411-002: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.0} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1mm} 0.45\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c|c|c} [2pt] \multirow{2}{*}{Dataset} \multirow{2}{*}{Model} \multicolumn{4}{c}{Metrics} {NDCG@5} {NDCG@10} {HR@5} {HR@10} \multirow{6}{*}{Beauty} {DIN} 0.2466 0.2684 0.3546 0.4223 {GRU4Rec} 0.2390 0.2692 0.3645 0.4571 {SASRec} 0.2512 0.2849 0.3854 0.4893 ComiSA 0.2594 {0.2783} {0.3842} {0.4813} {ComiDR} 0.2582 0.2925 {0.3833} 0.4902 \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}TTT4Rec \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.2571 \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.2890 \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.3866 \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.4848 \multirow{6}{*}{Electronic} {DIN} 0.3286 0.3664 0.4434 0.5603 {GRU4Rec} 0.3450 0.3844 0.4666 0.5882 {SASRec} 0.3420 0.3821 0.4641 0.5881 ComiSA {0.3065} {0.3301} {0.4221} {0.5418} ComiDR {0.3228} {0.3623} {0.4414} {0.5637} \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}{TTT4Rec} \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.3486 \textbf{\cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.3892} \cellcolor[HTML] {DCDCDC}0.4712 \cellcolor[HTML] {DCDCDC}0.5968 \multirow{6}{*}{ML-1m} {DIN} 0.4703 0.5081 0.6318 0.7485 {GRU4Rec} 0.5209 0.5566 0.6762 0.7863 {SASRec} 0.5217 0.5574 0.6790 0.7919 ComiSA {0.4040} {0.4450} {0.5417} {0.6982} {ComiDR} {0.4072} {0.4549} {0.5550} {0.7022} \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC} TTT4Rec \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.5239 \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.5577 \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.6834 \cellcolor[HTML]{DCDCDC}0.7877 [2pt] \end{tabular} } \caption{ Performance comparison of different methods. The best performance is highlighted in bold.} \label{table:main_table}
elements.sent-2411-028: Overall Performance: Table \ref{table:main_table summarizes the evaluations of TTT4Rec and baseline models across the datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-029: TTT4Rec matches or surpasses the baselines in NDCG and Hit metrics.
elements.sent-2411-030: While ComiRec performs better overall due to advanced techniques, its training efficiency is lower, requiring 50 epochs on the Beauty dataset to match the performance TTT4Rec achieves in a single epoch (Figure \ref{fig:ndcg ).}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{images/result.png} \caption{The NDCG@5 metrics for ComiSA and ComiDR evolve during training, with the dashed line showing TTT4Rec's performance at epoch 1 on the Beauty dataset. } \label{fig:ndcg}
elements.sent-2411-031: Hyper-parameter Analysis: In our experiments, we identified two parameters that had a significant impact on performance, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:hyper :.}
elements.sent-2411-032: A value that's too small leads to minimal parameter variation, impairing functionality, while a value that's too large makes the parameter space too broad, complicating the search for optimal parameters.
elements.sent-2411-033: The results suggest that optimal performance is achieved with batch sizes of 1 or 10, indicating that both online and batch gradient descent (GD) are more effective for TTT-Linear in this context.
elements.sent-2411-034: Mini-batch GD updates the current token using W derived from several tokens apart, which may link unrelated tokens and adversely affect performance.
elements.sent-2411-035: As a base model utilizing TTT-Linear for feature extraction, TTT4Rec has shown performance that is on par with, or even exceeds, other base models within the same environment.
elements.sent-2411-036: Future research will aim to further refine the parameter W in TTT-Linear.
elements.sent-2411-037: This work was in part supported by 2030 National Science and Technology Major Project (2022ZD0119100), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62402429, 62441605), the Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province (No. 2024C03270), ZJU Kunpeng\&Ascend Center of Excellence.
